skill.name: golang-lo
skill.description: Use samber/lo in Go 1.18 or newer projects to simplify collections, maps, strings, errors, retries, channels, and pointer handling.
skill.summary: Guides Go developers in applying samber/lo helpers for concise generic utility code.
skill.author: DCjanus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged imports, shell-style backticks, a URL, weak crypto, and high entropy. Review found these are Markdown examples and multilingual frontmatter, not executable skill code or malicious instructions. The only real risk is user-invoked package and network commands, which are expected for a Go library guide.

low_findings.title: User-Invoked Package And Network Commands
low_findings.description: The guide shows `go get github.com/samber/lo@v1` and `curl -sSL https://lo.samber.dev/llms.txt`. These commands can download code or remote documentation if a user runs them, but they are visible Markdown examples and match the skill purpose.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and URL are directly present in the skill file. They are not hidden or automatically executed, so the risk is limited to deliberate user action.

low_findings.title: Markdown Import And Backtick False Positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic import and Ruby-style backtick alerts map to a Go import block, Markdown code fences, and inline Markdown formatting. I did not find evidence of runtime script loading or shell execution by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows ordinary Markdown and Go sample code. There is no executable wrapper, interpreter directive, or instruction to run these snippets as shell commands.

low_findings.title: Crypto And Entropy Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to the frontmatter description, which discusses the samber/lo utility library and not cryptographic code. The entropy alert is consistent with short multilingual Markdown content, not encoded payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain frontmatter and Chinese text only. No cryptographic API, encoded blob, binary data, or suspicious payload appears in the reviewed file.

content.value_statement: Go collection and utility code can become verbose and repetitive. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code apply samber/lo patterns clearly.
actual_capabilities: Explains common samber/lo imports for standard, parallel, mutable, and iterator helpers.
Shows how to use Filter, Map, Find, Uniq, GroupBy, and Must in Go code.
Guides use of samber/lo for collections, maps, strings, errors, retries, channels, and pointers.
Points users to the upstream lo function list through a documented curl command.
Warns users to compare upstream function lists with their local dependency version.
limitations: It is a concise reference, not a full samber/lo manual.
It does not verify installed module versions automatically.
It does not execute tests or benchmark refactored Go code.
It depends on user review before adding package imports or network commands.

use_cases.title: Simplify Slice Processing
use_cases.description: Replace repetitive loops with clear samber/lo helpers while preserving behavior.

use_cases.title: Review Utility Library Adoption
use_cases.description: Check whether a Go codebase can benefit from lo helpers without harming readability.

use_cases.title: Learn Practical Go Generics
use_cases.description: Use real collection examples to understand generic helper functions in Go.

prompt_templates.title: Find Simple Refactors
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of repetitive Go collection code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Go function and suggest where samber/lo could make collection handling clearer. Explain each suggestion briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Loops Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Refactor existing loops with behavior preservation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite these Go loops using samber/lo where it improves clarity. Keep behavior unchanged and mention any cases you leave as loops.

prompt_templates.title: Choose The Right Helper
prompt_templates.scenario: Select helpers for a mixed collection task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this Go data transformation, choose suitable samber/lo helpers and explain why each helper fits the task.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A Migration Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before adopting samber/lo across a codebase.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this proposed samber/lo migration plan. Identify readability risks, version concerns, test needs, and places where plain Go is better.

output_examples.input: A Go function filters active users, maps their names, and removes duplicates with manual loops.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends Filter, Map, and Uniq, then explains when a plain loop may remain clearer.

output_examples.input: A team asks whether to use lo.Must for parsing configuration values.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that Must can fit initialization paths, but normal error handling is better for request-time parsing.

output_examples.input: A developer wants the latest samber/lo helper list before refactoring.
output_examples.output: The assistant points to the documented upstream list command and reminds the developer to check the installed module version.

best_practices: Use samber/lo where it improves readability, not only to reduce line count.
Check the installed lo version before using helpers from upstream documentation.
Keep tests around refactors that change collection, error, or concurrency behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not replace simple loops when the helper version is harder to read.
Do not use lo.Must in paths where errors should be returned to callers.
Do not run package or curl commands without reviewing the target and version.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps apply samber/lo helpers in Go projects that use Go 1.18 or newer.

faq.question: Does it install samber/lo automatically?
faq.answer: No. It shows the install command, but the user decides whether to run it.

faq.question: Can it replace every Go loop?
faq.answer: No. It should only replace loops when the result is clearer and well tested.

faq.question: Does it cover parallel helpers?
faq.answer: Yes. It references the parallel import path, but it does not provide a full concurrency guide.

faq.question: Is the upstream function list always safe to use?
faq.answer: No. The skill warns that the list may include changes not present in the local dependency version.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
